preamble: Iotation is a phonological process where an unstressed or weakened vowel /a/ loses its syllabic status and develops into the palatal glide [j], typically before a following high vowel or in hiatal resolution. This process is characteristic of Romance language evolution and occurs through reduced articulatory force and increased coarticulation with adjacent high vowels.

examples.note: Unstressed /a/ before /a/ develops secondary glide [j] through iotation, particularly in intervocalic position
The /a/ before stressed vowel weakens and palatalizes to [j], creating the common Spanish pattern -ay-
Through iotation and vowel coalescence, /a/ + /e/ develops a glide [j] in hiatal context

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology: Structural and Philological Aspects.
Grossmann, M., & Rainer, F. (Eds.). (2004). La formation des mots dans les langues romanes.

related.label: Deletion variant of iotation and weakening
